bridge.label: My Work
bridge.heading: Projects Built
bridge.description: Case studies in engineering decisions, honest tradeoffs, and systems built to last.

grid.label: Case Studies
grid.heading: Three projects. Three problems worth solving.
grid.description: Each one started with a real constraint and ended with something I learned from.

agrotech.title: AgroTech 2.0
agrotech.hook: A desktop application for smart agricultural irrigation — built in Java, powered by Arduino sensors, and recognized by UNINPAHU as the semester's standout project.
agrotech.challenge: Bogotá's urban agriculture sector lacked accessible tooling for precision irrigation. Smallholders were manually managing water schedules without real-time soil data, leading to overwatering and crop loss. The university challenge: build a working prototype that collects soil moisture data and automates irrigation decisions — within a single semester, with a team of three.
agrotech.approach: I led architecture decisions and divided responsibility by system layer — hardware integration went to the team member with electronics experience, UI to our designer, and I owned the Java application core and Arduino communication layer. We chose JavaFX for the desktop interface because it gave us full control without web overhead, and Maven for reproducible builds across our three machines. Arduino handled sensor polling; Java handled the decision logic.
agrotech.build: The application connects to an Arduino board reading soil moisture and temperature sensors in real time. A polling loop in Java fetches serial data every configurable interval, runs it through threshold-based irrigation logic, and triggers relay signals back to the Arduino to open or close a water valve. The JavaFX interface shows live sensor readings, irrigation history, and lets the user configure thresholds per crop type. Maven managed the serial communication library (jSerialComm) and ensured clean builds on all three developer machines.
agrotech.outcome: The project received university recognition in the first semester of 2024 — one of a small number of student projects flagged for academic distinction. The prototype worked end-to-end in a demo environment: sensor readings fed live into the UI, threshold logic triggered the relay correctly, and the interface was clean enough for a non-technical user to operate. The team dynamic held — the authorship was genuinely distributed, and the lead role taught me how to coordinate technical decisions under time pressure.
agrotech.learnings: I'd modularize the Arduino communication into a proper adapter layer from day one instead of letting serial I/O bleed into business logic. I'd also write at least basic unit tests for the threshold decision logic — it worked, but I couldn't prove it would hold under edge cases without manual testing. On the team side, I'd establish a shared Git workflow earlier; we lost time resolving merge conflicts that a simple branching convention would have prevented.

transmilenio-router.title: Transmilenio Router
transmilenio-router.hook: A graph-based route optimizer for Bogotá's TransMilenio BRT network — finds the shortest path through 141 stations using Dijkstra's algorithm, with a natural language query layer on top.
transmilenio-router.challenge: TransMilenio's official app gives you a route. It doesn't explain why, and it doesn't let you query in plain language. I wanted to understand both problems: can I model a real city transit network as a graph, and can I put a simple NLP interface on top of it so a user can ask 'how do I get from Portal Norte to Usme' and get a real answer? The network has 141 stations across multiple lines with transfer logic — not trivial to model correctly.
transmilenio-router.approach: Python was the right tool: NetworkX handles graph operations cleanly, and the ecosystem for NLP prototyping is fast. I modeled each station as a node and each direct connection as a weighted edge — weights representing estimated travel time. Dijkstra's algorithm via NetworkX finds the shortest path. The NLP layer uses fuzzy string matching to map free-text station names to graph nodes, so spelling variations don't break the query. v1 was a CLI tool. v2 is the current in-progress web interface.
transmilenio-router.build: The graph is built from a static dataset of TransMilenio stations and connections that I compiled and cleaned manually — that was the most time-consuming part. NetworkX ingests it at startup and provides O(E log V) shortest-path queries. The NLP preprocessing normalizes input (lowercasing, accent stripping, common abbreviation expansion) before fuzzy matching against the station name list using difflib. A threshold prevents false matches from returning garbage results. The CLI outputs the path as an ordered station list with stop count and estimated time. v2 adds a Flask API layer and a small React frontend.
transmilenio-router.outcome: v2 is live. The routing works correctly across the full network — a query from Portal Norte to Usme returns a 6-stop optimal path versus the 14-stop naive route a new commuter might take. The fuzzy matching handles real-world Bogotá Spanish variations well enough that I've yet to need manual correction in testing. It's not a product — it's a proof of concept — but it demonstrates that a city-scale transit graph is tractable with the right tooling.
transmilenio-router.learnings: The manual station dataset was the biggest time sink and the biggest fragility. I'd prioritize finding or scraping an authoritative GTFS feed from Transmilenio's published open data instead of building the graph by hand. I'd also separate the NLP matching from the routing logic earlier — they're two different problems and mixing them made testing harder. For v2, I want to add transfer penalties to the edge weights so the algorithm prefers fewer transfers over marginally shorter paths.

portfolio.title: This Portfolio
portfolio.hook: A bilingual, statically generated portfolio built on Next.js 15, Docker, and GCP — designed around a 'Blueprints Coming to Life' metaphor and deployed with a full CI/CD pipeline.
portfolio.challenge: Most developer portfolios are either generic templates or visual experiments that sacrifice readability. I wanted something that felt genuinely mine: technically rigorous, bilingual from the start, and with a design language that communicated the way I think — structured but not sterile. The constraint was also practical: it needed to run on a DigitalOcean droplet with 512MB RAM, meaning no heavy runtime overhead.
portfolio.approach: Next.js 15 App Router with full static generation was the foundation — every page is pre-rendered at build time, so the server only serves static assets at runtime. Tailwind CSS v4 with CSS custom properties gives the design system enough flexibility for the blueprint aesthetic without runtime CSS-in-JS cost. next-intl handles EN/ES routing with server-side translation loading. Docker + Nginx sit in front of everything, and GitHub Actions handles build, test, and deploy on every push to main.
portfolio.build: The design system is built on a set of CSS custom property tokens — never raw hex values in components. The 'Blueprints Coming to Life' metaphor manifests as a blueprint grid background, technical annotation overlays, gradient text transitions from cyan to coral, and ghost monogram watermarks on section backgrounds. Each page is a thin orchestrator that composes server and client components — server components handle static markup and translation loading, client components handle animation and interaction. Framer Motion drives scroll reveals with a custom `useInView` hook tuned to handle scroll-up and reload-at-bottom edge cases.
portfolio.outcome: The site is live at bccloudsolutions.dev. Build times are under 30 seconds. The Docker image is lean enough for the 512MB droplet with swap headroom. The EN/ES toggle works correctly across all static paths. The design holds up in both light and dark mode. Each page sprint has followed a spec-driven process — decisions locked before implementation, no scope creep mid-sprint.
portfolio.learnings: I'd define the full CSS token system before writing any component — I had to retroactively normalize some color usages in early components when the token naming convention solidified mid-sprint. I'd also set up the i18n namespace structure on day one; adding namespaces late meant re-touching components to update import paths. The deployment pipeline is solid, but I'd add a staging environment earlier in the next project to catch build-time errors before they hit production.
